title: Paradise Lost - Book IV
content:
В В В В O for that warning voice, which he who saw
В В В В ThпїЅ Apocalyps, heard cry in Heaven aloud,
В В В В Then when the Dragon, put to second rout,
В В В В Came furious down to be revengпїЅd on men,
В В В В Wo to the inhabitants on Earth! that now,
В В В В While time was, our first Parents had bin warnd
В В В В The coming of thir secret foe, and scapпїЅd
В В В В Haply so scapпїЅd his mortal snare; for now
В В В В Satan, now first inflamпїЅd with rage, came down,
В В В В The Tempter ere thпїЅ Accuser of man-kind,
В В В В To wreck on innocent frail man his loss
В В В В Of that first Battel, and his flight to Hell:
В В В В Yet not rejoycing in his speed, though bold,
В В В В Far off and fearless, nor with cause to boast,
В В В В Begins his dire attempt, which nigh the birth
В В В В Now rowling, boiles in his tumultuous brest,
В В В В And like a devillish Engine back recoiles
В В В В Upon himself; horror and doubt distract
В В В В His troublпїЅd thoughts, and from the bottom stirr
В В В В The Hell within him, for within him Hell
В В В В He brings, and round about him, nor from Hell
В В В В One step no more then from himself can fly
В В В В By change of place: Now conscience wakes despair
В В В В That slumberd, wakes the bitter memorie
В В В В Of what he was, what is, and what must be
В В В В Worse; of worse deeds worse sufferings must ensue.
В В В В Sometimes towards Eden which now in his view
В В В В Lay pleasant, his grievd look he fixes sad,
В В В В Sometimes towards HeavпїЅn and the full-blazing Sun,
В В В В Which now sat high in his Meridian Towre:
В В В В Then much revolving, thus in sighs began.
В В В В O thou that with surpassing Glory crownd,
В В В В LookпїЅst from thy sole Dominion like the God
В В В В Of this new World; at whose sight all the Starrs
В В В В Hide thir diminisht heads; to thee I call,
В В В В But with no friendly voice, and add thy name
В В В В O Sun, to tell thee how I hate thy beams
В В В В That bring to my remembrance from what state
В В В В I fell, how glorious once above thy Spheare;
В В В В Till Pride and worse Ambition threw me down
В В В В Warring in HeavпїЅn against HeavпїЅns matchless King:
В В В В Ah wherefore! he deservd no such return
В В В В From me, whom he created what I was
В В В В In that bright eminence, and with his good
В В В В Upbraided none; nor was his service hard.
В В В В What could be less then to afford him praise,
В В В В The easiest recompence, and pay him thanks,
В В В В How due! yet all his good provпїЅd ill in me,
В В В В And wrought but malice; lifted up so high
В В В В I sdeind subjection, and thought one step higher
В В В В Would set me highest, and in a moment quit
В В В В The debt immense of endless gratitude,
В В В В So burthensome, still paying, still to ow;
В В В В Forgetful what from him I still receivd,
В В В В And understood not that a grateful mind
В В В В By owing owes not, but still pays, at once
В В В В Indebted and dischargd; what burden then?
В В В В O had his powerful Destiny ordaind
В В В В Me some inferiour Angel, I had stood
В В В В Then happie; no unbounded hope had raisпїЅd
В В В В Ambition. Yet why not? som other Power
В В В В As great might have aspirпїЅd, and me though mean
В В В В Drawn to his part; but other Powers as great
В В В В Fell not, but stand unshakпїЅn, from within
В В В В Or from without, to all temptations armпїЅd.
В В В В Hadst thou the same free Will and Power to stand?
В В В В Thou hadst: whom hast thou then or what to accuse,
В В В В But HeavпїЅns free Love dealt equally to all?
В В В В Be then his Love accurst, since love or hate,
В В В В To me alike, it deals eternal woe.
В В В В Nay cursпїЅd be thou; since against his thy will
В В В В Chose freely what it now so justly rues.
В В В В Me miserable! which way shall I flie
В В В В Infinite wrauth, and infinite despaire?
В В В В Which way I flie is Hell; my self am Hell;
В В В В And in the lowest deep a lower deep
В В В В Still threatning to devour me opens wide,
В В В В To which the Hell I suffer seems a HeavпїЅn.
В В В В O then at last relent: is there no place
В В В В Left for Repentance, none for Pardon left?
В В В В None left but by submission; and that word
В В В В Disdain forbids me, and my dread of shame
В В В В Among the spirits beneath, whom I seducпїЅd
В В В В With other promises and other vaunts
В В В В Then to submit, boasting I could subdue
В В В В ThпїЅ Omnipotent. Ay me, they little know
В В В В How dearly I abide that boast so vaine,
В В В В Under what torments inwardly I groane;
В В В В While they adore me on the Throne of Hell,
В В В В With Diadem and Scepter high advancпїЅd
В В В В The lower still I fall, onely Supream
В В В В In miserie; such joy Ambition findes.
В В В В But say I could repent and could obtaine
В В В В By Act of Grace my former state; how soon
В В В В Would highth recal high thoughts, how soon unsay
В В В В What feignпїЅd submission swore: ease would recant
В В В В Vows made in pain, as violent and void.
В В В В For never can true reconcilement grow
В В В В Where wounds of deadly hate have peircпїЅd so deep:
В В В В Which would but lead me to a worse relapse
В В В В And heavier fall: so should I purchase deare
В В В В Short intermission bought with double smart.
В В В В This knows my punisher; therefore as farr
В В В В From granting hee, as I from begging peace:
В В В В All hope excluded thus, behold in stead
В В В В Of us out-cast, exilпїЅd, his new delight,
В В В В Mankind created, and for him this World.
В В В В So farwel Hope, and with Hope farwel Fear,
В В В В Farwel Remorse: all Good to me is lost;
В В В В Evil be thou my Good; by thee at least
В В В В Divided Empire with HeavпїЅns King I hold
В В В В By thee, and more then half perhaps will reigne;
В В В В As Man ere long, and this new World shall know.
В В В В Thus while he spake, each passion dimmпїЅd his face
В В В В Thrice changпїЅd with pale, ire, envie and despair,
В В В В Which marrd his borrowпїЅd visage, and betraid
В В В В Him counterfet, if any eye beheld.
В В В В For heavпїЅnly mindes from such distempers foule
В В В В Are ever cleer. Whereof hee soon aware,
В В В В Each perturbation smoothпїЅd with outward calme,
В В В В Artificer of fraud; and was the first
В В В В That practisd falshood under saintly shew,
В В В В Deep malice to conceale, couchпїЅt with revenge:
В В В В Yet not anough had practisd to deceive
В В В В Uriel once warnd; whose eye pursuпїЅd him down
В В В В The way he went, and on thпїЅ Assyrian mount
В В В В Saw him disfigurпїЅd, more then could befall
В В В В Spirit of happie sort: his gestures fierce
В В В В He markd and mad demeanour, then alone,
В В В В As he supposпїЅd, all unobservпїЅd, unseen.
В В В В So on he fares, and to the border comes
В В В В Of Eden, where delicious Paradise,
В В В В Now nearer, Crowns with her enclosure green,
В В В В As with a rural mound the champain head
В В В В Of a steep wilderness, whose hairie sides
В В В В With thicket overgrown, grottesque and wilde,
В В В В Access deniпїЅd; and over head up grew
В В В В Insuperable highth of loftiest shade,
В В В В Cedar, and Pine, and Firr, and branching Palm,
В В В В A Silvan Scene, and as the ranks ascend
В В В В Shade above shade, a woodie Theatre
В В В В Of stateliest view. Yet higher then thir tops
В В В В The verdurous wall of Paradise up sprung:
В В В В Which to our general Sire gave prospect large
В В В В Into his neather Empire neighbouring round.
В В В В And higher then that Wall a circling row
В В В В Of goodliest Trees loaden with fairest Fruit,
В В В В Blossoms and Fruits at once of golden hue
В В В В Appeerd, with gay enameld colours mixt:
В В В В On which the Sun more glad impressпїЅd his beams
В В В В Then in fair Evening Cloud, or humid Bow,
В В В В When God hath showrd the earth; so lovely seemd
В В В В That Lantskip: And of pure now purer aire
В В В В Meets his approach, and to the heart inspires
В В В В Vernal delight and joy, able to drive
В В В В All sadness but despair: now gentle gales
В В В В Fanning thir odoriferous wings dispense
В В В В Native perfumes, and whisper whence they stole
В В В В Those balmie spoiles. As when to them who saile
В В В В Beyond the Cape of Hope, and now are past
В В В В Mozambic, off at Sea North-East windes blow
В В В В Sabean Odours from the spicie shoare
В В В В Of Arabie the blest, with such delay
В В В В Well pleasпїЅd they slack thir course, and many a League
В В В В Cheard with the grateful smell old Ocean smiles.
В В В В So entertaind those odorous sweets the Fiend
В В В В Who came thir bane, though with them better pleasпїЅd
В В В В Then Asmodeus with the fishie fume,
В В В В That drove him, though enamourd, from the Spouse
В В В В Of Tobits Son, and with a vengeance sent
В В В В From Media post to пїЅgypt, there fast bound.
В В В В Now to thпїЅ ascent of that steep savage Hill
В В В В Satan had journied on, pensive and slow;
В В В В But further way found none, so thick entwinпїЅd,
В В В В As one continuпїЅd brake, the undergrowth
В В В В Of shrubs and tangling bushes had perplext
В В В В All path of Man or Beast that past that way:
В В В В One Gate there onely was, and that lookпїЅd East
В В В В On thпїЅ other side: which when thпїЅ arch-fellon saw
В В В В Due entrance he disdaind, and in contempt,
В В В В At one slight bound high overleapпїЅd all bound
В В В В Of Hill or highest Wall, and sheer within
В В В В Lights on his feet. As when a prowling Wolfe,
В В В В Whom hunger drives to seek new haunt for prey,
В В В В Watching where Shepherds pen thir Flocks at eeve
В В В В In hurdlпїЅd Cotes amid the field secure,
В В В В Leaps oпїЅre the fence with ease into the Fould:
В В В В Or as a Thief bent to unhoord the cash
В В В В Of some rich Burgher, whose substantial dores,
В В В В Cross-barrd and bolted fast, fear no assault,
В В В В In at the window climbes, or oпїЅre the tiles;
В В В В So clomb this first grand Thief into Gods Fould:
В В В В So since into his Church lewd Hirelings climbe.
В В В В Thence up he flew, and on the Tree of Life,
В В В В The middle Tree and highest there that grew,
В В В В Sat like a Cormorant; yet not true Life
В В В В Thereby regaind, but sat devising Death
В В В В To them who livпїЅd; nor on the vertue thought
В В В В Of that life-giving Plant, but only usпїЅd
В В В В For prospect, what well usпїЅd had bin the pledge
В В В В Of immortalitie. So little knows
В В В В Any, but God alone, to value right
В В В В The good before him, but perverts best things
В В В В To worst abuse, or to thir meanest use.
В В В В Beneath him with new wonder now he views
В В В В To all delight of human sense exposпїЅd
В В В В In narrow room Natures whole wealth, yea more,
В В В В A Heaven on Earth, for blissful Paradise
В В В В Of God the Garden was, by him in the East
В В В В Of Eden planted; Eden stretchd her Line
В В В В From Auran Eastward to the Royal Towrs
В В В В Of great Seleucia, built by Grecian Kings,
В В В В Or where the Sons of Eden long before
В В В В Dwelt in Telassar: in this pleasant soile
В В В В His farr more pleasant Garden God ordaind;
В В В В Out of the fertil ground he causпїЅd to grow
В В В В All Trees of noblest kind for sight, smell, taste;
В В В В And all amid them stood the Tree of Life,
В В В В High eminent, blooming Ambrosial Fruit
В В В В Of vegetable Gold; and next to Life
В В В В Our Death the Tree of knowledge grew fast by,
В В В В Knowledge of Good bought dear by knowing ill.
В В В В Southward through Eden went a River large,
В В В В Nor changпїЅd his course, but through the shaggie hill
В В В В PassпїЅd underneath ingulft, for God had thrown
В В В В That Mountain as his Garden mould high raisпїЅd
В В В В Upon the rapid current, which through veins
В В В В Of porous Earth with kindly thirst up drawn,
В В В В Rose a fresh Fountain, and with many a rill
В В В В Waterd the Garden; thence united fell
В В В В Down the steep glade, and met the neather Flood,
В В В В Which from his darksom passage now appeers,
В В В В And now divided into four main Streams,
В В В В Runs divers, wandring many a famous Realme
В В В В And Country whereof here needs no account,
В В В В But rather to tell how, if Art could tell,
В В В В How from that Saphire Fount the crisped Brooks,
В В В В Rowling on Orient Pearl and sands of Gold,
В В В В With mazie error under pendant shades
В В В В Ran Nectar, visiting each plant, and fed
В В В В Flours worthy of Paradise which not nice Art
В В В В In Beds and curious Knots, but Nature boon
В В В В Powrd forth profuse on Hill and Dale and Plaine,
В В В В Both where the morning Sun first warmly smote
В В В В The open field, and where the unpiercпїЅt shade
В В В В Imbround the noontide Bowrs: Thus was this place,
В В В В A happy rural seat of various view;
В В В В Groves whose rich Trees wept odorous Gumms and Balme,
В В В В Others whose fruit burnisht with Golden Rinde
В В В В Hung amiable, Hesperian Fables true,
В В В В If true, here onely, and of delicious taste:
В В В В Betwixt them Lawns, or level Downs, and Flocks
В В В В Grasing the tender herb, were interposпїЅd,
В В В В Or palmie hilloc, or the flourie lap
В В В В Of som irriguous Valley spread her store,
В В В В Flours of all hue, and without Thorn the Rose:
В В В В Another side, umbrageous Grots and Caves
В В В В Of coole recess, oпїЅre which the mantling Vine
В В В В Layes forth her purple Grape, and gently creeps
В В В В Luxuriant; mean while murmuring waters fall
В В В В Down the slope hills, disperst, or in a Lake,
В В В В That to the fringed Bank with Myrtle crownd,
В В В В Her chrystall mirror holds, unite thir streams.
В В В В The Birds thir quire apply; aires, vernal aires,
В В В В Breathing the smell of field and grove, attune
В В В В The trembling leaves, while Universal Pan
В В В В Knit with the Graces and the Hours in dance
В В В В Led on thпїЅ Eternal Spring. Not that faire field
В В В В Of Enna, where Proserpin gathring flours
В В В В Her self a fairer Floure by gloomie Dis
В В В В Was gatherd, which cost Ceres all that pain
В В В В To seek her through the world; nor that sweet Grove
В В В В Of Daphne by Orontes, and thпїЅ inspirпїЅd
В В В В Castalian Spring might with this Paradise
В В В В Of Eden strive; nor that Nyseian Ile
В В В В Girt with the River Triton, where old Cham,
В В В В Whom Gentiles Ammon call and Libyan Jove,
В В В В Hid Amalthea and her Florid Son
В В В В Young Bacchus from his Stepdame RheaпїЅs eye;
В В В В Nor where Abassin Kings thir issue Guard,
В В В В Mount Amara, though this by som supposпїЅd
В В В В True Paradise under the Ethiop Line
В В В В By Nilus head, enclosпїЅd with shining Rock,
В В В В A whole dayes journey high, but wide remote
В В В В From this Assyrian Garden, where the Fiend
В В В В Saw undelighted all delight, all kind
В В В В Of living Creatures new to sight and strange:
В В В В Two of far nobler shape erect and tall,
В В В В Godlike erect, with native Honour clad
В В В В In naked Majestie seemd Lords of all,
В В В В And worthie seemd, for in thir looks Divine
В В В В The image of thir glorious Maker shon,
В В В В Truth, Wisdome, Sanctitude severe and pure,
В В В В Severe, but in true filial freedom placпїЅt;
В В В В Whence true autoritie in men; though both
В В В В Not equal, as thir sex not equal seemd;
В В В В For contemplation hee and valour formd,
В В В В For softness shee and sweet attractive Grace,
В В В В Hee for God only, shee for God in him:
В В В В His fair large Front and Eye sublime declarпїЅd
В В В В Absolute rule; and Hyacinthin Locks
В В В В Round from his parted forelock manly hung
В В В В Clustring, but not beneath his shoulders broad:
В В В В Shee as a vail down to the slender waste
В В В В Her unadorned golden tresses wore
В В В В Dissheveld, but in wanton ringlets wavпїЅd
В В В В As the Vine curles her tendrils, which impliпїЅd
В В В В Subjection, but requirпїЅd with gentle sway,
В В В В And by her yeilded, by him best receivd,
В В В В Yeilded with coy submission, modest pride,
В В В В And sweet reluctant amorous delay.
В В В В Nor those mysterious parts were then conceald,
В В В В Then was not guiltie shame, dishonest shame
В В В В Of natures works, honor dishonorable,
В В В В Sin-bred, how have ye troublпїЅd all mankind
В В В В With shews instead, meer shews of seeming pure,
В В В В And banisht from mans life his happiest life,
В В В В Simplicitie and spotless innocence.
В В В В So passd they naked on, nor shund the sight
В В В В Of God or Angel, for they thought no ill:
В В В В So hand in hand they passd, the lovliest pair
В В В В That ever since in loves imbraces met,
В В В В Adam the goodliest man of men since borne
В В В В His Sons, the fairest of her Daughters Eve.
В В В В Under a tuft of shade that on a green
В В В В Stood whispering soft, by a fresh Fountain side
В В В В They sat them down, and after no more toil
В В В В Of thir sweet Gardning labour then sufficпїЅd
В В В В To recommend coole Zephyr, and made ease
В В В В More easie, wholsom thirst and appetite
В В В В More grateful, to thir Supper Fruits they fell,
В В В В Nectarine Fruits which the compliant boughes
В В В В Yeilded them, side-long as they sat recline
В В В В On the soft downie Bank damaskt with flours:
В В В В The savourie pulp they chew, and in the rinde
В В В В Still as they thirsted scoop the brimming stream;
В В В В Nor gentle purpose, nor endearing smiles
В В В В Wanted, nor youthful dalliance as beseems
В В В В Fair couple, linkt in happie nuptial League,
В В В В Alone as they. About them frisking playd
В В В В All Beasts of thпїЅ Earth, since wilde, and of all chase
В В В В In Wood or Wilderness, Forrest or Den;
В В В В Sporting the Lion rampd, and in his paw
В В В В DandlпїЅd the Kid; Bears, Tygers, Ounces, Pards
В В В В Gambold before them, thпїЅ unwieldy Elephant
В В В В To make them mirth usпїЅd all his might, and wreathd
В В В В His Lithe Proboscis; close the Serpent sly
В В В В Insinuating, wove with Gordian twine
В В В В His breaded train, and of his fatal guile
В В В В Gave proof unheeded; others on the grass
В В В В Coucht, and now fild with pasture gazing sat,
В В В В Or Bedward ruminating: for the Sun
В В В В DeclinпїЅd was hasting now with prone carreer
В В В В To thпїЅ Ocean Iles, and in thпїЅ ascending Scale
В В В В Of HeavпїЅn the Starrs that usher Evening rose:
В В В В When Satan still in gaze, as first he stood,
В В В В Scarce thus at length faild speech recoverd sad.
В В В В O Hell! what doe mine eyes with grief behold,
В В В В Into our room of bliss thus high advancпїЅt
В В В В Creatures of other mould, earth-born perhaps,
В В В В Not Spirits, yet to heavпїЅnly Spirits bright
В В В В Little inferior; whom my thoughts pursue
В В В В With wonder, and could love, so lively shines
В В В В In them Divine resemblance, and such grace
В В В В The hand that formd them on thir shape hath pourd.
В В В В Ah gentle pair, yee little think how nigh
В В В В Your change approaches, when all these delights
В В В В Will vanish and deliver ye to woe,
В В В В More woe, the more your taste is now of joy;
В В В В Happie, but for so happie ill securпїЅd
В В В В Long to continue, and this high seat your HeavпїЅn
В В В В Ill fencпїЅt for HeavпїЅn to keep out such a foe
В В В В As now is enterd; yet no purposпїЅd foe
В В В В To you whom I could pittie thus forlorne
В В В В Though I unpittied: League with you I seek,
В В В В And mutual amitie so streight, so close,
В В В В That I with you must dwell, or you with me
В В В В Henceforth; my dwelling haply may not please
В В В В Like this fair Paradise, your sense, yet such
В В В В Accept your Makers work; he gave it me,

В В В В To entertain you two, her widest Gates,
В В В В And send forth all her Kings; there will be room,
В В В В Not like these narrow limits, to receive
В В В В Your numerous ofspring; if no better place,
В В В В Thank him who puts me loath to this revenge
В В В В On you who wrong me not for him who wrongd.
В В В В And should I at your harmless innocence

В В В В Honour and Empire with revenge enlargпїЅd,

В В В В The Tyrants plea, excusпїЅd his devilish deeds.
В В В В Then from his loftie stand on that high Tree

В В В В Of those fourfooted kindes, himself now one,
В В В В Now other, as thir shape servd best his end
В В В В Neerer to view his prey, and unespiпїЅd
В В В В To mark what of thir state he more might learn
В В В В By word or action markt: about them round
В В В В A Lion now he stalkes with fierie glare,

В В В В To first of women Eve thus moving speech,
В В В В Turnd him all eare to heare new utterance flow.
В В В В Sole partner and sole part of all these joyes,
В В В В Dearer thy self then all; needs must the Power
В В В В That made us, and for us this ample World
В В В В Be infinitly good, and of his good
В В В В As liberal and free as infinite,

В В В В Aught whereof hee hath need, hee who requires
В В В В From us no other service then to keep
В В В В This one, this easie charge, of all the Trees
В В В В In Paradise that beare delicious fruit
В В В В So various, not to taste that onely Tree

В В В В God hath pronouncпїЅt it death to taste that Tree,
В В В В The only sign of our obedience left
В В В В Among so many signes of power and rule
В В В В Conferrd upon us, and Dominion givпїЅn
В В В В Over all other Creatures that possesse
В В В В Earth, Aire, and Sea. Then let us not think hard
В В В В One easie prohibition, who enjoy
В В В В Free leave so large to all things else, and choice
В В В В Unlimited of manifold delights:
В В В В But let us ever praise him, and extoll
В В В В His bountie, following our delightful task

В В В В Which were it toilsom, yet with thee were sweet.
В В В В To whom thus Eve repliпїЅd. O thou for whom
В В В В And from whom I was formd flesh of thy flesh,
В В В В And without whom am to no end, my Guide
В В В В And Head, what thou hast said is just and right.
В В В В For wee to him indeed all praises owe,
В В В В And daily thanks, I chiefly who enjoy
В В В В So farr the happier Lot, enjoying thee
В В В В PrпїЅeminent by so much odds, while thou
В В В В Like consort to thy self canst no where find.
В В В В That day I oft remember, when from sleep
В В В В I first awakпїЅt, and found my self reposпїЅd
В В В В Under a shade on flours, much wondring where
В В В В And what I was, whence thither brought, and how.
В В В В Not distant far from thence a murmuring sound
В В В В Of waters issuпїЅd from a Cave and spread
В В В В Into a liquid Plain, then stood unmovпїЅd
В В В В Pure as thпїЅ expanse of HeavпїЅn; I thither went
В В В В With unexperiencпїЅt thought, and laid me downe
В В В В On the green bank, to look into the cleer
В В В В Smooth Lake, that to me seemd another Skie.
В В В В As I bent down to look, just opposite,
В В В В A Shape within the watry gleam appeerd
В В В В Bending to look on me, I started back,
В В В В It started back, but pleasd I soon returnd,
В В В В PleasпїЅd it returnd as soon with answering looks
В В В В Of sympathie and love, there I had fixt
В В В В Mine eyes till now, and pinпїЅd with vain desire,
В В В В Had not a voice thus warnd me, What thou seest,
В В В В What there thou seest fair Creature is thy self,
В В В В With thee it came and goes: but follow me,
В В В В And I will bring thee where no shadow staies
В В В В Thy coming, and thy soft imbraces, hee
В В В В Whose image thou art, him thou shall enjoy
В В В В Inseparablie thine, to him shalt beare
В В В В Multitudes like thy self, and thence be callпїЅd
В В В В Mother of human Race: what could I doe,
В В В В But follow strait, invisibly thus led?
В В В В Till I espiпїЅd thee, fair indeed and tall,
В В В В Under a Platan, yet methought less faire,
В В В В Less winning soft, less amiablie milde,
В В В В Then that smooth watry image; back I turnd,
В В В В Thou following crydпїЅst aloud, Return fair Eve,
В В В В Whom fliпїЅst thou? whom thou fliпїЅst, of him thou art,
В В В В His flesh, his bone; to give thee being I lent
В В В В Out of my side to thee, neerest my heart
В В В В Substantial Life, to have thee by my side
В В В В Henceforth an individual solace dear;
В В В В Part of my Soul I seek thee, and thee claim
В В В В My other half: with that thy gentle hand
В В В В Seisd mine, I yeilded, and from that time see
В В В В How beauty is excelld by manly grace
В В В В And wisdom, which alone is truly fair.
В В В В So spake our general Mother, and with eyes
В В В В Of conjugal attraction unreprovпїЅd,
В В В В And meek surrender, half imbracing leand
В В В В On our first Father, half her swelling Breast
В В В В Naked met his under the flowing Gold
В В В В Of her loose tresses hid: he in delight
В В В В Both of her Beauty and submissive Charms
В В В В SmilпїЅd with superior Love, as Jupiter
В В В В On Juno smiles, when he impregns the Clouds

В В В В Sight hateful, sight tormenting! thus these two
В В В В ImparadisпїЅt in one anothers arms

В В В В Of bliss on bliss, while I to Hell am thrust,
В В В В Where neither joy nor love, but fierce desire,
В В В В Among our other torments not the least,
В В В В Still unfulfillпїЅd with pain of longing pines;
В В В В Yet let me not forget what I have gainпїЅd

В В В В One fatal Tree there stands of Knowledge callпїЅd,

В В В В Suspicious, reasonless. Why should thir Lord
В В В В Envie them that? can it be sin to know,
В В В В Can it be death? and do they onely stand
В В В В By Ignorance, is that thir happie state,
В В В В The proof of thir obedience and thir faith?
В В В В O fair foundation laid whereon to build
В В В В Thir ruine! Hence I will excite thir minds
В В В В With more desire to know, and to reject
В В В В Envious commands, invented with designe

В В В В Equal with Gods; aspiring to be such,
В В В В They taste and die: what likelier can ensue?

В В В В This Garden, and no corner leave unspiпїЅd;
В В В В A chance but chance may lead where I may meet
В В В В Some wandring Spirit of HeavпїЅn, by Fountain side,

В В В В What further would be learnt. Live while ye may,
В В В В Yet happie pair; enjoy, till I return,
В В В В Short pleasures, for long woes are to succeed.
В В В В So saying, his proud step he scornful turnпїЅd,
В В В В But with sly circumspection, and began
В В В В Through wood, through waste, oпїЅre hil, oпїЅre dale his roam.

В В В В With Earth and Ocean meets, the setting Sun

В В В В Of Alablaster, pilпїЅd up to the Clouds,
В В В В Conspicuous farr, winding with one ascent
В В В В Accessible from Earth, one entrance high;
В В В В The rest was craggie cliff, that overhung
В В В В Still as it rose, impossible to climbe.

В В В В ThпїЅ unarmed Youth of HeavпїЅn, but nigh at hand

В В В В Gabriel, to thee thy course by Lot hath givпїЅn
В В В В Charge and strict watch that to this happie place
В В В В No evil thing approach or enter in;
В В В В This day at highth of Noon came to my Spheare
В В В В A Spirit, zealous, as he seemпїЅd, to know
В В В В More of thпїЅ Almighties works, and chiefly Man

В В В В But in the Mount that lies from Eden North,

В В В В Uriel, no wonder if thy perfet sight,

В В В В When Adam thus to Eve: Fair Consort, thпїЅ hour
В В В В Of night, and all things now retirпїЅd to rest

В В В В Labour and rest, as day and night to men
В В В В Successive, and the timely dew of sleep
В В В В Now falling with soft slumbrous weight inclines
В В В В Our eye-lids; other Creatures all day long

В В В В Man hath his daily work of body or mind
В В В В Appointed, which declares his Dignitie,

В В В В Tomorrow ere fresh Morning streak the East

В В В В Ask riddance, if we mean to tread with ease;

В В В В To whom thus Eve with perfet beauty adornd.
В В В В My Author and Disposer, what thou bidst
В В В В UnarguпїЅd I obey; so God ordains,
В В В В God is thy Law, thou mine: to know no more
В В В В Is womans happiest knowledge and her praise.
В В В В With thee conversing I forget all time,
В В В В All seasons and thir change, all please alike.
В В В В Sweet is the breath of morn, her rising sweet,
В В В В With charm of earliest Birds; pleasant the Sun
В В В В When first on this delightful Land he spreads
В В В В His orient Beams, on herb, tree, fruit, and flour,
В В В В Glistring with dew; fragrant the fertil earth
В В В В After soft showers; and sweet the coming on

В В В В But neither breath of Morn when she ascends
В В В В With charm of earliest Birds, nor rising Sun
В В В В On this delightful land, nor herb, fruit, floure,
В В В В Glistring with dew, nor fragrance after showers,
В В В В Nor grateful Evening mild, nor silent Night
В В В В With this her solemn Bird, nor walk by Moon,

В В В В But wherfore all night long shine these, for whom
В В В В This glorious sight, when sleep hath shut all eyes?
В В В В To whom our general Ancestor repliпїЅd.
В В В В Daughter of God and Man, accomplisht Eve,
В В В В Those have thir course to finish, round the Earth,
В В В В By morrow Eevning, and from Land to Land

В В В В These then, though unbeheld in deep of night,
В В В В Shine not in vain, nor think, though men were none,

В В В В Millions of spiritual Creatures walk the Earth
В В В В Unseen, both when we wake, and when we sleep:
В В В В All these with ceasless praise his works behold

В В В В While they keep watch, or nightly rounding walk
В В В В With HeavпїЅnly touch of instrumental sounds
В В В В In full harmonic number joind, thir songs

В В В В Thus talking hand in hand alone they passпїЅd
В В В В On to thir blissful Bower; it was a place
В В В В ChosпїЅn by the sovran Planter, when he framпїЅd
В В В В All things to mans delightful use; the roofe
В В В В Of thickest covert was inwoven shade

В В В В RearпїЅd high thir flourisht heads between, and wrought
В В В В Mosaic; underfoot the Violet,

В В В В Of costliest Emblem: other Creature here

В В В В More sacred and sequesterd, though but feignd,
В В В В Pan or Silvanus never slept, nor Nymph,
В В В В Nor Faunus haunted. Here in close recess

В В В В And heavпїЅnly Quires the HymenпїЅan sung,

В В В В Brought her in naked beauty more adornпїЅd,

В В В В Endowd with all thir gifts, and O too like

В В В В Both turnd, and under opпїЅn Skie adorпїЅd
В В В В The God that made both Skie, Air, Earth and HeavпїЅn

This evocative piece by John Milton represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.
